item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
It's possible it was something like this thread.

http://www.scpwiki.com/forum/t-470400/a-collection-of-scp-like-images

I suppose I could try to recreate the image to the best of my ability, if that's what's required to remain compliant.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I can't source it at this point, but when I first wrote this entry I went through some SCP image source archive and stumbled upon that photo to use as an inspiration for an object. I don't know where that collection of images is now, but does anyone know if that was a thing in 2012, or if it still exists?

All I know is, a GRIS of the photo returns nothing but references to this page. I don't recall editing the image at the time, but I suppose it's possible.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi.

I liked this scp. Detail and tone were good, commitment to write some longer testing logs is good and it not needing to be 'scary' was good.

Why is it not simply buried pointing downwards? Subterranean weather systems would be interesting. Effect on aquifers, underground rivers or lakes? May be worth exploring.

Thanks for writing this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that's neat. As someone who lives in the Pacific Northwest, Oregon especially, frequently has short bouts of odd weather and no shortage of wind. I will always have a soft spot for Scips that make me take something in my day to day that I find odd and give them a "explanation" for them. This will be in the back of my mind anytime we have the weather abruptly change which, for those who live here all know, is all the time. +1'd

P.S. Sorry for such a late post, I just started reading through all these things a little bit ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, but that's boring. I suppose I'll have to figure out a reason why we're not doing that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't a better containment procedure be to have it pointed straight up? That would result in a much smaller area that they need to keep people out of, and a much smaller volume of air where anomalous weather can happen (since the air will rapidly become too thin to have much weather in it).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test logs dragged on for far too long and overall I found the skip boring.

item.title: Re: Surprised this doesn't have more +1's
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I guess it just isn't immediately interesting enough. I should probably try writing another SCP sometime…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually had the Red Spot in mind with

storm bore a strong resemblance to [DATA EXPUNGED]

But doesn't exactly "transmit" weather from other planets, though it has the capability of reproducing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That circular storm thing made me curious (it and the aforementioned toxic gas and odd cloud color). Does this weathervane transmit weather resembling a source not on this planet (i.e. does this thing cause Jupiter's weather to manifest on earth), or does it just cause weird stuff with no pattern?

item.title: Surprised this doesn't have more +1's
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, but even better implementation in my opinion. The logs are what make this great, I think. For me, it's definitely deserving of a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found that page and picked the unused number 37.
Edit: I was looking at site 28, not sector 28. Maybe that'll work.

As for the post below… The research has been done and the change has been made. Unless there's a strong reason to KEEP the blacking, I'll leave it as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blacking out Site numbers is fine: the common logic being that that the only people who need to know where it is are the people who can do something from a distance and the people who are already there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/secure-facilities-locations

Sector-28 is a coastal facility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the reason for blacking out the number is because I'm not sure what site numbers already exist with defined locations and didn't want to cause any conflicts. And for the sake of everything making sense, I felt I needed to say that it's on a coast somewhere.

Maybe I'll just pick a number, do a quick search of the site, and see if that number is used anywhere. Then I can leave the site number and location un-blacked out. Would that help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'located in a remote location on the ██████ coast'
I think that's not needed, given you mention the site, and black out the number. If you don't want them to know the number, no reason to state it's on a coast. And whoever knows the number, should know where the site is.
Other than that, not bad~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know that feel, bro. I also like what you did here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair point. That, of course, is what happens when parts get added later and I don't review the previous content close enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The testing logs were well written, and the effects well elucidated. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If we're using the USCG to keep people out of the affected zone, then it seems a little pointless to say it's pointing "due west toward the ███████ Ocean". To my knowledge, we don't have many Coast Guard operations running in, say, the Indian Ocean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I wasn't sure how to make it interesting without getting too over the top.
I'll fix the spelling errors, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except for the said spelling errors, this is very good in my opinion. The concept doesn't leave me in a state of amazement, yes, but the execution is topnotch. Good work on the testing logs - they provide good back-up and greatly improve the overall quality.

Браво! (Bravo!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't particularly grab me in any way. There are a couple spelling errors though. Really can't say much else about it.
